item.content:encoded: I noticed a lot of weird things like "site 19" and "sector 34", as well as some… unusual formatting? And overall, it just wasn't that enjoyable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Utterly impossible to understand without reading the very detailed explanation written by the author in the comments, I really can't support that kind of skips. Downvoted.

item.title: Re: I just came here to say...
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you liked the video and the article, it means a lot!

Hope to see more.

I'm replying to you two months later, because I've just started working on a new entry and logged in for the first time in months. So if you like "following" specific contributors to the wiki and want more stuff from me specifically, stay tuned!

item.title: Re: I just came here to say...
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for linking that! Truly awe-inspiring. I had a friend for years that was a mathematics researcher (don't ask me his focus, even if I remembered it I wouldn't understand it), and he managed to explain some of his work to my dumb ass just enough for me to be thoroughly creeped out and filled with a kind of cosmic dread.

Great article, by the way. Again, you captured a great sense of awe. Hope to see more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This may be my new go-to for most underrated article. The combination of plausible SF and plausible human conflict is unlike anything else on the wiki.

item.title: Re: I just came here to say...
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! If you've not seen it before, you will enjoy this video about the Monster Group (featuring John Conway!): https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jsSeoGpiWsw

The way mathematicians talk about it as if it boggles their mind, almost with fear and respect, gives me strong SCP vibes.

item.title: I just came here to say...
item.content:encoded:
I fricking love seeing actual physics and math on SCP. This is a beautiful anomaly and the story is quietly heartbreaking.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've expanded it with an addendum! I think it ties up some loose ends and clarifies what's going on behind the scenes. Let me know if there's a mistake regarding what has been established of the inner workings of the Foundation…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Flatland to Earth:

Hi.

Pass it on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fucking love this. Does an excellent job of making the Foundation a bit more realistic, in that resources are scarce, bureaucracy is hell, and pedantic arguments are inevitable when you bring very smart people with large egos together. The object itself is not all that interesting but the backstory of the two researchers and the second-hand aspects of containment are phenomenal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"196,000 dimensional beings"

Jesus that's a lot of dimensions

wonder what it looks like up there.

Also, you pulled off the technobabble amazingly. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks!

In retrospect I agree I might have got carried away with a few ideas and tried too hard to make them work together. The whole "edit wars" thing is not that important, but it started organically and I got attached to it :

  • I tried to make the "Special Containment Procedures" section more intriguing with the backstory about the disastrous excavation process that lead them to just condemn a whole area to stop disturbing it ASAP
  • I was so glad with the way it explored the "logistic" side of managing anomalous items that I doubled down on the attempted realism and tried to develop what decades of containment of a harmless thing would entail
  • I started to add a bunch of addenda to create a feeling of accumulated changes in containment policies over decades
  • I got interested in the idea that it would start being neglected, and felt like it would be really different to make the scariness come from the Foundation ignoring important stuff
  • From then on I needed another point of view to emphasize that the item is very important despite being just a harmless rock
  • I got caught up into the side-story about the in-fighting within the Foundation, and started putting as much effort into developing both the item itself and the side-story.

So this whole process made me way too attached to the "edit wars" aspect, maybe I should strongly reduce it and keep it aside for another entry. I'll think about it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the premise you describe here, but I see no compelling reason to bog the article down and make it even harder to access the already-obscure concept by introducing a narrative about scientific controversy that has such a heavy effect on the article.

The factual, scientific core of the article is really cool, but the puzzle of piecing it together through all the pollution is far more difficult than it is rewarding. I'd say, save that concept for an easier-to-understand article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, I thought of a reason why they would want to destroy the thing. What if it has potentially dangerous SCPs like 2317 that would release unless the destroyed the universes?

Anyway, I get it better now. Thank you! Although the points I made (about something anomalously bad happening when destroyed/something anomalously bad happening when living) should maybe be considered if you're ever going to do changes to this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well the rock contains trillions of parallel instances of this universe (and as far as they know most atoms in all kinds of matter contain similar structures), and they've been dying one by one since the twenties through natural causes. This item has no interaction with occult stuff or property you wouldn't expect from any rock… My idea is that pretty much every quark contains a universe, so it was probable that at some point, one would develop through completely natural means, just like life on Earth did, a civilisation so advanced it managed to send a signal at our scale, but (but of course that implies acting within its universe without breaking the laws of physics by basically getting it to vibrate in a preset pattern, destroying all life within it in the process. They can't "get outside" any more than we can get outside our universe). And the Foundation has done decades of research since its discovery to confirm all of this. Nothing about that makes it a potential threat. And on top of that, the bulk of the message has already been recorded twice. So the case that that this item could be abandoned without consequences seems strong to me!

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Great. You're set then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this…. is this a debate? Are… what? The people at the Foundation really don't think, with all the unexpected anomalies in the world, that the death of a fucking universe inside another universe won't cause negative consequences?

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Done! I used this image as the background, which is under CC0 license (I can modify it and use it for any purpose without asking for permission, it's completely public domain).

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: For safety's sake, use something you know is compliant. It's always the best choice.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: I made both images, the second one entirely from scratch. The first one is a picture I took of a rock, photoshopped over a background photo which I admit is not mine, I can't find the source but it's the ceiling of the USS Truman hangar deck. I think the background is mostly hidden and all in all the picture is "transformative"/unrecognizable enough not to be infringement. But I can understand if you're not convinced or don't want to take the risk. If needed I can find a background I'm sure I'm free to use, or just keep the extremely altered bottom part with the "X-ray" effect.

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page. If the images are not wholly your own, please post the image's source as a link (or links) in reply to this post and edit it into your first post in this discussion thread.

Timer expired.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You skippified Whoville…as a SCIENCE! article no less.
I love it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot going on, I'm not sure what exactly you don't get so I'll try to restate the main points a bit more clearly and with less technical jargon:

  • The rock happens to contain a quartz crystal which, through random chance, crystallized in very homogeneous conditions. The result is that at a sub-atomic level, all the atoms are extremely similar. In this fiction, quarks (which are subatomic particles) have a chance of containing something similar to our universe at a very small scale (because within a quark, there can be tiny variations just like our galaxies and stars are just tiny pockets of energy in the void of space). I tried to make sure all that stuff is somewhat scientifically plausible, no "magic" involved!
  • Since an atom can contain a tiny universe, neighbouring atoms that are very similar can all individually generate a universe and the result will be very similar! So like parallel universes or alternate realities. This is what happened inside this rock.
  • There is an extremely small chance of life being born, and then civilisation being born, and then this civilisation becoming extremely advanced, and then this civilisation trying and succeeding to send a message to an higher plane of existence in a given universe. But since there are trillions of trillions of atoms in the Earth, it was bound to happen eventually somewhere! This rock is amongst the ones in which it happened, and since there are a lot of "copies" of the universe, the message is detectable even though a message from a single atom would of course be undetectable.
  • This civilisation has a lot figured out about reality, and they understand that beyond their two dimensions, and beyond OUR three dimensions, the plane of existence that is really special and really matters has 196,884 dimensions (this is supported by actual math! There is something about this amount of dimensions which is different and nobody understands exactly why, it just IS, embedded in the way numbers work. Spooky stuff.) Since they're a post-scarcity civilisation that has been freed long ago from animalistic desires and has explored and discovered everything, they want to contact those high-dimensional beings of course. But since they're only two-dimensional and trapped inside a tiny quark, they need US to carry their message across. Of course the only way would be to alter our universe in such a way that it would be visible to higher, presumably four-dimensional beings. So just destroy our universe to make it vibrate in the right pattern, exactly like they did to send us electromagnetic waves!
  • The Foundation is starting to understand this. Of course this is a "Safe", harmless item. But think of all the knowledge it contains! Unfortunately it is quite fragile since the tiniest disturbance has an effect on every atom and obviously the extremely small patterns that are their universes cannot withstand perturbations at our huge scale. Since the crystal is inside a thick layer of granite a few billions managed to survive anyway, but the signal is decreasing and the rock requires a lot of space and maintenance to ensure it decays as slowly as possible to keep recording the message.
  • The Foundation has a bunch of eldritch abominations to take care of though, and sinking a lot of money into maintaining this boring rock might seem a tad wasteful to some people. Thus two opposite sides arise: those who want to keep studying the rock and learn stuff from an extremely advanced civilisation that seems to have solved the fundamental nature of reality, and those who think the Foundation should use resources to stop Cthulhu instead of keeping a chunk of stone well-insulated for decades (not to mention that everything in the message seems aimed at getting us to destroy our universe to transmit the message to a higher realm).
  • The controversy is so deep that both sides start using "propaganda" of sorts (while staying within the rules of the Foundation) to advance their agenda. Since technical documentation should stay neutral, but pretty much nobody amongst those who contribute to SCP documents is neutral on this issue, you're getting a bunch of edits from both points of view within the document (Supported by facts, as technical documentation should! But both sides are backed by undeniable facts.). I intended the reader to piece together the whole story through those glimpses at the internal conflict that pollute the factual, scientific core of the document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And all the Whos down in Whoville let out a radio death cry as their universe crumbled…

Really liked the passive-aggressive back and forth between the researchers in the notes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The technobabble is completely impenetrable, but I at least get a sense of "civilization of tiny, two-dimensional things trying to contact higher dimensions in the most complicated way possible" as the implication.

I'm taking this mostly as an interesting experiment in tone. The way this is written is wholly unique, slightly frustrating, but quite charming. +1 in the hopes this sticks around and gets better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm of the opinion that you have to slap the reader in the face with your point in order for them to get it. It doesn't have to be in the whole article, but at some point, if you want people to understand, the reader must be able to see something behind the curtain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you!

I was really torn about whether to include the collapsible note from Director Jones or not… But in the feedback I got on an earlier draft someone pointed out that the personnel directly editing SCP containment procedures felt really wrong, and there must be a strict review system with the higher ups having the final say. The whole thing also seemed a bit too subtle without a very clear explanation that you're getting two opposing points of view mashed into a document that's meant to stay "neutral". I wanted to tone down the impression that the very clusterfucky feeling was unintentional, you're meant to experience the conflict through the leftover subjective parts that managed to stay neutral and well-referenced enough to get approved. But in this version I flat out explain that there are revisions right at the beginning, so maybe it gets a bit redundant.

By tonal issues do you mean the bits of comic relief with the pettiness of the two head researchers/the most passive-aggressive footnotes? I can see how they might feel wrong and not really funny.

About development, I'm afraid that the Foundation having figured out too much about the message and the entities' intents would weaken the story… An important part of the story is the sense that the message is important but fading away and very difficult to figure out because it's from extremely advanced beings. Giving too much away would deter from the feeling that the item is still worth preserving and make the budget-conscious side clearly in the right, making the whole conflict pointless. But maybe you're right, there's probably room for a bit more information without crossing that line, just one piece of information still missing could be enough to warrant the conflict.

item.title: Re: Wow
item.content:encoded:
Thanks!

All the technobabble related to physics actually has meaning, although it might describe impossible things but I tried to make it scientifically accurate (as long as you accept there can be structure within quarks, which I think doesn't violate any laws of physics as long as the patterns "cancel out" so the quark flavour stays the same). The maths is not arbitrary either, the 'Monster Group' is a fascinating real thing.

The containment procedures are somewhat random though I have to admit, especially the part about the protective tiling. I just like the idea of a readily available supply of cheap 'multipurpose' deflection panels that includes a bunch of protective features, just for anomalies that are not completely figured out yet, and it seemed like a good way to drive the point home that resources are primarily allocated to dangerous items, leaving the harmless items a bit neglected.

I might have overused precise numerical values though (I felt like giving the number of bits in the message and the durations was something you would find in a technical description, but of course it glosses over many other stuff that could have numerical values so maybe I could ditch a few more specific numbers).

The document references are meant to show how the two competing schools of thought try to make their revisions hard to reject, and to avoid giving the impression that the Foundation would allow revisions that are not supported by facts… But I see how it can be annoying to read. That's how real-life scientists fight each other though as far as I know!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the idea for a work in progress SCP document and the font thing is particularly clever, but the description collapsible and some tonal issues are holding it back. I also think some additional development would serve this SCP well. I also noticed a few capitalization errors. -1 for now but I think this has potential.

item.title: Wow
item.content:encoded:
Just wow. That's a really great (and actually quite sad) idea for a SCP.

+1

I'm not a fan of the huge amount of technobabble, though, and this and constant references to other documents makes it really hard to follow at some times.

EDIT : although in this case, the constant technobabble and references gives the skip a particular style I can't really describe, it feels different from other skips, and I like it.
